caseStudies.title: Sengkang Ltd Financial Statements
caseStudies.text:
The following financial statements are provided for Sengkang Ltd for the year ended 20X3.

BALANCE SHEET (in $'000):
                          20X3      20X2
Cash                      3,290     4,220
Accounts receivable       8,200     7,350
Inventories              14,000    13,860
Total current assets     25,490    25,430
PP&E (net)               34,380    30,660
Total assets             59,870    56,090

Accounts payable         11,560    11,980
Interest-bearing liabilities 19,880  18,900
Total liabilities        31,440    30,880
Share capital (15,400K shares) 15,400  15,400
Retained earnings        13,030     9,810
Total equity             28,430    25,210

INCOME STATEMENT 20X3 (in $'000):
Revenue (net sales)      110,000
Cost of sales             70,200
Gross profit              39,800
Selling & distribution    14,200
Administrative             9,940
Finance costs              3,120
Total expenses            27,260
Profit before tax         12,540
Tax (30%)                  3,816
Profit after tax           8,724

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION:
- Depreciation: $18,750K
- Tax paid: $3,816K
- Market price per ordinary share: $60
- Shares outstanding: 15,400,000
- Dividends paid: $5,504K

caseStudies.title: Fairfield Manufacturing — Cost Classification and CVP
caseStudies.text:
Fairfield Manufacturing produces a single product. Management requires assistance with cost classification, contribution margin analysis, and break-even calculations to support planning decisions.

Selected data for the period:
- Selling price per unit: $50
- Variable cost per unit: $30
- Total fixed costs: $80,000
- Actual units sold: 6,000
- Budgeted units: 5,500

caseStudies.title: Budget Variance Analysis
caseStudies.text:
A company prepared a static budget for the period but actual results differed. Management requires a flexible budget and variance analysis to understand performance.

Static budget data:
- Budgeted units: 10,000
- Budgeted selling price: $25 per unit
- Budgeted variable cost: $15 per unit
- Budgeted fixed costs: $50,000

Actual results:
- Actual units sold: 12,000
- Actual revenue: $288,000
- Actual variable costs: $186,000
- Actual fixed costs: $52,000

caseStudies.title: Journal Entries and Adjustments
caseStudies.text:
A company undertakes several transactions during the period. You are required to record these using the double-entry system, prepare adjusting entries at period end, and calculate depreciation.

Transactions:
a. Purchased equipment for $60,000 cash (5-year life, zero residual, straight-line)
b. Purchased supplies on credit for $3,000; $800 remains unused at period end
c. Received $12,000 in advance for services to be performed over 6 months; 2 months have elapsed
d. Accrued wages of $4,500 unpaid at period end
e. An insurance policy was prepaid for $6,000 covering 12 months; 3 months have elapsed

questions.label: Question 1a
questions.text: Calculate the following ratios for Sengkang Ltd for the financial year 20X3. Show all workings and compare each ratio against the given industry average.

subItems.label: (ii)
subItems.text: Return on ordinary shareholders' equity (Industry average: 20.0%)

questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Return on Total Assets (ROA)
Formula: Net Profit / Average Total Assets × 100
Average Total Assets = (59,870 + 56,090) / 2 = 115,960 / 2 = $57,980K
ROA = 8,724 / 57,980 × 100 = 15.05%
Comparison: 15.05% > Industry avg 12.0% — ABOVE industry average (favourable)

(ii) Return on Ordinary Shareholders' Equity (ROE)
Formula: Net Profit / Average Shareholders' Equity × 100
Average Equity = (28,430 + 25,210) / 2 = 53,640 / 2 = $26,820K
ROE = 8,724 / 26,820 × 100 = 32.53%
Comparison: 32.53% > Industry avg 20.0% — ABOVE industry average (favourable)

(iii) Gross Margin
Formula: Gross Profit / Revenue × 100
Gross Margin = 39,800 / 110,000 × 100 = 36.18%
Comparison: 36.18% < Industry avg 38.0% — BELOW industry average (unfavourable)

(iv) Current Ratio
Formula: Current Assets / Current Liabilities
Current Ratio = 25,490 / 11,560 = 2.20×
Comparison: 2.20× > Industry avg 2.0× — ABOVE industry average (favourable)

(v) Earnings Per Share (EPS)
Formula: Net Profit / Number of Ordinary Shares
EPS = $8,724,000 / 15,400,000 shares = $0.57 per share
keyPoints: ROA uses average total assets (average of opening and closing balances)
ROE uses average shareholders' equity (average of opening and closing balances)
Gross margin uses gross profit (revenue minus cost of sales), not net profit
Current ratio uses total current liabilities ($11,560K = accounts payable only shown as current)
EPS converts $'000 to actual dollars before dividing by number of shares
Always state whether ratio is above/below industry average and whether favourable/unfavourable

questions.label: Question 1b
questions.text: Based on the ratios calculated in Question 1a, comment on Sengkang Ltd's overall financial performance. Your answer should address profitability, liquidity, and any areas of concern.
questions.modelAnswer:
Overall Assessment of Sengkang Ltd's Financial Performance:

PROFITABILITY:
- ROA of 15.05% exceeds the industry average of 12.0%, indicating that Sengkang is generating above-average returns from its asset base. This suggests efficient asset utilisation.
- ROE of 32.53% significantly exceeds the industry average of 20.0%, demonstrating strong returns for shareholders and effective use of equity financing.
- However, gross margin of 36.18% is below the industry average of 38.0%, indicating that Sengkang's cost of sales is relatively high compared to peers. Management should investigate whether this is due to higher input costs, pricing pressure, or inefficiencies in production.

LIQUIDITY:
- Current ratio of 2.20× exceeds the industry average of 2.0×, suggesting adequate short-term liquidity. The company can comfortably meet its current obligations.

OVERALL:
- Sengkang Ltd shows strong overall financial performance, outperforming the industry in profitability and liquidity metrics. The one area of concern is the below-average gross margin, which if not addressed, could erode net profitability over time.
- The company should focus on managing its cost of sales to improve gross margin towards or above the industry benchmark.
keyPoints: Comment on each ratio in relation to its industry average
Address both profitability (ROA, ROE, gross margin) and liquidity (current ratio)
Identify the weakness: gross margin below industry average
Provide specific recommendations or areas for management attention
Use ratio values in your discussion, not just vague statements

questions.label: Question 1c
questions.text:
Calculate the following investor ratios for Sengkang Ltd for 20X3:
(i) Price-Earnings (P/E) ratio
(ii) Dividend yield
Interpret what each ratio tells investors about the company.
questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Price-Earnings (P/E) Ratio
Formula: Market Price per Share / Earnings per Share
EPS (from Q1a) = $0.57
P/E Ratio = $60 / $0.57 = 105.26×
Interpretation: Investors are paying $105.26 for every $1 of earnings. This high P/E suggests investors expect significant future earnings growth, or the stock may be overvalued relative to current earnings.

(ii) Dividend Yield
Formula: Dividend per Share / Market Price per Share × 100
Dividend per Share (DPS) = Total Dividends / Number of Shares
DPS = $5,504,000 / 15,400,000 = $0.357 per share
Dividend Yield = $0.357 / $60 × 100 = 0.60%
Interpretation: Investors receive $0.60 for every $100 invested in the form of dividends. This is a very low dividend yield, suggesting the company retains most of its earnings for reinvestment (growth company), or investors are primarily seeking capital gains rather than dividend income.
keyPoints: P/E ratio uses EPS calculated in Q1a ($0.57)
DPS = Total dividends paid / number of shares (convert $'000 to actual dollars)
Dividend yield is expressed as a percentage
Always interpret what the ratio means for investors
Low dividend yield is typical of growth companies that reinvest earnings

questions.label: Question 2a
questions.text:
For Fairfield Manufacturing, classify each of the following costs as: (1) Fixed or Variable, and (2) Direct or Indirect. Provide a brief justification for each classification.

Costs to classify:
(i) Raw materials used in production
(ii) Factory supervisor's salary
(iii) Electricity for factory machinery (varies with production)
(iv) Depreciation on factory building (straight-line)
(v) Sales commissions paid per unit sold
questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Raw materials
- Fixed/Variable: VARIABLE — cost increases directly with each unit produced
- Direct/Indirect: DIRECT — can be directly traced to the specific product

(ii) Factory supervisor's salary
- Fixed/Variable: FIXED — salary does not change with production volume
- Direct/Indirect: INDIRECT — cannot be traced to a specific unit; it is a manufacturing overhead

(iii) Electricity for factory machinery
- Fixed/Variable: VARIABLE — consumption rises and falls with production activity
- Direct/Indirect: INDIRECT — difficult to trace directly to individual units; treated as overhead

(iv) Depreciation on factory building (straight-line)
- Fixed/Variable: FIXED — remains constant regardless of production level
- Direct/Indirect: INDIRECT — a period cost allocated as manufacturing overhead

(v) Sales commissions
- Fixed/Variable: VARIABLE — paid per unit sold, so increases with sales volume
- Direct/Indirect: INDIRECT — selling expense, not a product cost; cannot be traced to individual units in the same way direct materials can
keyPoints: Variable costs change in total with volume; fixed costs remain constant in total
Direct costs can be traced to the product; indirect costs cannot and are allocated
Raw materials and direct labour are the main direct costs
Factory overheads (supervisor salary, depreciation, utilities) are indirect
Selling commissions are variable but are period costs, not product costs

questions.label: Question 2b
questions.text:
Using the data for Fairfield Manufacturing:
(i) Calculate the contribution margin per unit and contribution margin ratio.
(ii) Calculate the break-even point in units and in sales dollars.
(iii) Calculate the margin of safety in units and as a percentage.

subItems.label: (i)
subItems.text: Contribution margin per unit and contribution margin ratio

subItems.label: (ii)
subItems.text: Break-even point in units and in sales dollars

subItems.label: (iii)
subItems.text: Margin of safety in units and as a percentage

questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Contribution Margin
Contribution Margin per unit = Selling Price – Variable Cost per unit
= $50 – $30 = $20 per unit

Contribution Margin Ratio (CM Ratio) = CM per unit / Selling Price × 100
= $20 / $50 × 100 = 40%

(ii) Break-Even Point
Break-Even Units = Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin per unit
= $80,000 / $20 = 4,000 units

Break-Even Sales = Break-Even Units × Selling Price
= 4,000 × $50 = $200,000
Alternatively: Fixed Costs / CM Ratio = $80,000 / 0.40 = $200,000

(iii) Margin of Safety
Margin of Safety (units) = Actual Units – Break-Even Units
= 6,000 – 4,000 = 2,000 units

Margin of Safety (%) = Margin of Safety Units / Actual Units × 100
= 2,000 / 6,000 × 100 = 33.33%

Interpretation: Sales can fall by 33.33% before the company reaches break-even.
keyPoints: Contribution margin = Selling price minus variable costs only (not fixed costs)
Break-even uses FIXED costs divided by contribution margin per unit
CM ratio allows break-even to be calculated in dollars directly
Margin of safety shows how much sales can decline before a loss is made
Always show formula, substitution, and final answer with units

questions.label: Question 3a
questions.text: Using the budget and actual data provided, prepare a flexible budget for the actual level of activity (12,000 units) and calculate all variances. Indicate whether each variance is Favourable (F) or Unfavourable (U).
questions.modelAnswer:
FLEXIBLE BUDGET AT 12,000 UNITS:

Revenue:
Flexible Budget Revenue = 12,000 × $25 = $300,000
Actual Revenue = $288,000
Revenue Variance = $288,000 – $300,000 = –$12,000 (U)
(Actual selling price = $288,000/12,000 = $24 vs budgeted $25)

Variable Costs:
Flexible Budget Variable Costs = 12,000 × $15 = $180,000
Actual Variable Costs = $186,000
Variable Cost Variance = $186,000 – $180,000 = $6,000 (U)

Fixed Costs:
Budgeted Fixed Costs = $50,000
Actual Fixed Costs = $52,000
Fixed Cost Variance = $52,000 – $50,000 = $2,000 (U)

SUMMARY TABLE:
                    Flexible Budget   Actual    Variance
Revenue             $300,000          $288,000  $12,000 (U)
Variable Costs      $180,000          $186,000   $6,000 (U)
Contribution Margin $120,000          $102,000  $18,000 (U)
Fixed Costs          $50,000           $52,000   $2,000 (U)
Operating Profit     $70,000           $50,000  $20,000 (U)

Note: The static budget profit = (10,000 × $25) – (10,000 × $15) – $50,000 = $250,000 – $150,000 – $50,000 = $50,000
Sales volume variance = Flexible budget profit – Static budget profit = $70,000 – $50,000 = $20,000 (F) (due to higher volume)
keyPoints: Flexible budget recalculates variable costs at the actual volume (12,000 units)
Fixed costs remain the same in the flexible budget
Revenue variance is unfavourable because actual price ($24) < budgeted price ($25)
Variable cost variance is unfavourable because actual cost > flexible budget cost
Sales volume variance is favourable because actual volume > budgeted volume
Always label variances as F (Favourable) or U (Unfavourable)

questions.label: Question 3b
questions.text:
Explain the possible causes for:
(i) The unfavourable revenue variance
(ii) The unfavourable variable cost variance
(iii) The favourable sales volume variance
For each, suggest ONE management action to address the issue.
questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Unfavourable Revenue Variance ($12,000 U)
Causes: The actual selling price of $24 was lower than the budgeted price of $25. This could be due to:
- Increased market competition forcing price reductions
- Discounts offered to secure higher sales volume
- Weaker market demand than anticipated
Management Action: Review pricing strategy; consider whether volume discounts are justified by contribution margin analysis. Conduct market analysis to set competitive yet profitable prices.

(ii) Unfavourable Variable Cost Variance ($6,000 U)
Causes: Actual variable costs ($15.50/unit) exceeded budget ($15/unit). Possible causes:
- Increase in raw material prices
- Higher labour costs (overtime or wage increases)
- Production inefficiencies or wastage
Management Action: Negotiate better supplier contracts, review production processes for waste reduction, and consider bulk purchasing to reduce per-unit material costs.

(iii) Favourable Sales Volume Variance ($20,000 F)
Causes: Actual units sold (12,000) exceeded budget (10,000) by 2,000 units. Possible reasons:
- Successful marketing campaign
- Competitor supply disruption boosting demand
- Lower selling price attracting more customers
Management Action: Investigate whether higher volume was driven by the price reduction — if so, ensure the incremental revenue from extra volume justifies the lower price. Seek to sustain volume growth without sacrificing margin.
keyPoints: Link each variance back to a specific cause with a logical explanation
Note the connection between low price (revenue variance U) and high volume (volume variance F)
Management actions should be specific and actionable
Distinguish between price effects and efficiency/volume effects

questions.label: Question 4a
questions.text: Prepare journal entries for all transactions listed in the case study. Include adjusting entries at period end where applicable. Show debit and credit accounts clearly.
questions.modelAnswer:
JOURNAL ENTRIES:

(a) Purchase of equipment for cash:
DR Equipment                  $60,000
   CR Cash                             $60,000
(Being purchase of equipment)

(b) Purchase of supplies on credit:
DR Supplies (asset)            $3,000
   CR Accounts Payable                  $3,000
(Being purchase of supplies on credit)

Adjusting entry — supplies used = $3,000 – $800 = $2,200:
DR Supplies Expense            $2,200
   CR Supplies                          $2,200
(Being supplies consumed during period)

(c) Cash received in advance:
DR Cash                       $12,000
   CR Unearned Revenue                 $12,000
(Being cash received for services not yet performed)

Adjusting entry — 2 months earned = 2/6 × $12,000 = $4,000:
DR Unearned Revenue            $4,000
   CR Service Revenue                   $4,000
(Being recognition of revenue earned)

(d) Accrued wages:
DR Wages Expense               $4,500
   CR Wages Payable                     $4,500
(Being accrual of unpaid wages at period end)

(e) Prepaid insurance adjustment — 3 months elapsed = 3/12 × $6,000 = $1,500:
DR Insurance Expense           $1,500
   CR Prepaid Insurance                 $1,500
(Being insurance expense for 3 months recognised)

DEPRECIATION (for equipment in transaction a):
Annual depreciation = $60,000 / 5 = $12,000 per year
Assuming monthly = $12,000 / 12 = $1,000 per month
DR Depreciation Expense        $1,000
   CR Accumulated Depreciation          $1,000
(Being monthly depreciation on equipment)
keyPoints: Every transaction has equal debits and credits
Advance receipts are liabilities (Unearned Revenue) until earned
Adjusting entries are required for prepayments, accruals, and depreciation
Supplies: record the unused amount as asset, expense the used portion
Accruals record expenses incurred but not yet paid
Depreciation: always credit Accumulated Depreciation, not the asset account directly
